modules.name: SummaryEvery case is different. There isn’t o...
modules.description: Summary Every case is different. There isn’t one set way to solve an ethical dilemma. Use your judgment and consider your values to determine the best choice in a given situation. Embrace challenges. We avoid ethical decision-making because it’s hard. Try conditioning your brain to welcome challenges by seeing them as opportunities to grow, just as you’d condition your muscles to lift heavier weights at the gym. The more we practice overcoming difficulties, the easier it gets. Don't feed the fire. Negative gossip is like a fire—the more fuel you add, the bigger it gets. Instead of fuel, gossip survives on attention. Withdraw that, and negative gossip will fizzle out. When in doubt, cite your source. Protect yourself from the consequences of plagiarism by giving credit if you're unsure. It’s better to be safe than sorry. When in doubt, dig deeper. It’s easy to fall into motivated ignorance. All it takes is doing nothing. Instead, find out what’s really going on and tackle the problem di

modules.name: SummaryAre you making excuses? If you’re stru...
modules.description: Summary Are you making excuses? If you’re struggling to determine whether or not something is ethical, ask yourself: “Am I making excuses?” and “Am I trying to justify this?” Excuses are a red flag that your conscience doesn’t agree with your behavior. Listen to that inner voice—it reveals a lot about what’s right and wrong. Integrity requires consistency. If there’s one thing to take away from this lesson, remember: Integrity requires consistency. You cannot be a person of integrity if you waver on your values. You must do the right thing even when it’s hard and inconvenient. You must continue to make the right choices over and over again. Focus on the future. While it’s important to reflect on past patterns, don’t get stuck in a loop of regret. If you’ve done things you’re not proud of, recognize those tendencies, and then move forward. Evaluate each new decision and—before stepping forward—ask yourself, “Is this the next right move?” When in doubt—ask for help. I f you’re ever unsur

modules.name: SummaryReflect on your personal role models. ...
modules.description: Summary Reflect on your personal role models. Who do you look up to? What traits or aspects do you admire about them? Consider cultivating and incorporating those qualities in your personal practices and values. Review and update your code of conduct regularly. Establish a regular cadence for reviewing and updating your employee code of conduct. That way, it will always stay relevant. Have the right expectations. It’s not possible to predict every challenge your employees will face, so don’t get caught up trying to anticipate every potential issue. Instead, focus on informing, educating, and motivating your team to be mindful of the choices they’re making. Details matter. Ethical behavior doesn’t have to involve grand gestures. Helping out a customer or voicing concern about a policy that seems contrary to a company’s values are small-scale ways to “do the right thing.” We can all integrate ethics into our daily life by taking these kinds of actions. modules.name: SummaryDon’t cut corners. It’s easy to let sa...
modules.description: Summary Don’t cut corners. It’s easy to let safety slide. But cutting corners isn’t worth potentially fatal consequences. The next time you’re at a site tempted to “Just get the job done,” force yourself to pause. Slow down, assess the situation, and consider what you must do to stay safe. Your future self will thank you. Ask the experts. The front-line team knows better than anyone where risks lie. Get their input for improving workplace safety. Their knowledge and creative solutions make it a win-win for everyone. Report hazards to your manager. If you can’t remove a hazard yourself, report it promptly to your manager or human resources. Consult the pros. Always follow the manufacturer’s directions for storage, cleaning, and care of your personal protective eyewear. Visit OSHA.gov and ANSI.org to ensure you are following the correct safety protocols. Practice basic ladder maintenance. Help keep ladders in good condition with proper cleaning, care, and storage.

modules.name: SummaryStay vigilant. Be aware of your surrou...
modules.description: Summary Stay vigilant. Be aware of your surroundings at all times. Speak to a supervisor or HR rep immediately if you believe there is a threat to anyone’s safety. Disclaimer: This lesson was designed to overview potential risk factors for violence. It should not be used as a diagnostic tool. Workplace bullying happens in remote offices too. The privacy of instant messages and emails makes it easy for people to bully others online. The effects are devastating, even if those involved work remotely. Be aware of cyberbullying—it’s just as harmful as bullying in an in-person workspace. Refer to an EAP. An employee assistance program (EAP) can support employees dealing with aggressive tendencies and those affected by it. Mental health assistance provides meaningful intervention and skill-building to improve the likelihood of positive outcomes. Take care of yourself. modules.description: Summary Note: Determine organizational readiness before pursuing inclusive conversations. Is your organization truly ready to facilitate these conversations in a healthy, respectful, and safe way? If not, ask, what are some steps that we could take right now? And start there. Some organizations may be ready to fully dive into inclusive conversations, while others should start with baby steps. Choose the approach that works best for your employees. Your co-worker may not even realize they’ve said something offensive. While it’s easy to jump to conclusions when someone’s said something hurtful, it can be helpful to ask questions to clarify their message and intention. Forgive your mistakes. It’s normal to feel down about our mistakes but remember: No one is perfect. So, forgive yourself the next time your good intentions fall short. With so much diversity in the world, disconnects are inevitable. Rather than stressing about what you did, forgive yourself so you can focus on what you’ll d

modules.name: SummaryCultural competence is a moral and ethical ...
modules.description: Summary Cultural competence is a moral and ethical obligation. Research shows that diversity and multiculturalism are good for business, but organizations must think beyond the bottom line to create a workplace that celebrates all individuals and provides a safe and supportive place where all employees can flourish, personally and professionally. Focus on similarities. It’s easy to get overwhelmed by cultural differences and everything that could go wrong. But by emphasizing shared values, traditions, and insights, you’ll bridge the gap rather than widen it. Doing so will broaden your organization’s global appeal. Note: Recognize power and privilege. Class, race, nationality, language, gender identity, sexual orientation, and even marital status can all play a role in your relative privilege and power in a given situation. A culturally sensitive person is aware of that dynamic. Note: In a business setting, the best way to avoid committing cultural appropriation is by ensuring all voice

modules.name: SummaryActions speak louder than words. Psych...
modules.description: Summary Actions speak louder than words. Psychological safety in the workplace starts with individuals, then teams, and spreads throughout the organization. Checking in on colleagues, sharing your personal stories, and asking for and offering help are a few ways to build trust and practice what you preach. Inclusion depends on both systemic and individual efforts. It’s a manager’s job to ensure employees have the support they need to be consciously inclusive at work. Note: You don’t have to understand everything to be respectful. Fully understanding the gender spectrum can be difficult, especially for those who need to unlearn old norms before accepting new ones. But regardless of any individual’s knowledge level, they should strive to see, hear, and respect others. Speak up. If you notice instances of gender bias at work, voice your concerns. Go to your manager or human resources representative to begin a dialogue. Biases take root because of complacency and inaction. Raising awarenes

modules.name: SummaryTame your stress with laughter. When stress...
modules.description: Summary Tame your stress with laughter. When stress gets the better of us, it can be hard to let loose and laugh. But there’s a reason why people say, “Laughter is the best medicine.” Laughter can reduce your stress hormones, soothe tension, improve your immune system, and relieve pain. Even a single hearty laugh can leave your muscles relaxed for up to 45 minutes afterward. To create more moments for laughter, watch a funny movie, read and share a joke, play a board game, and surround yourself with fun-loving people. Don’t avoid stress completely. While it’s important to keep your stress level low, in some cases, stress can be helpful. In manageable doses, it can motivate us to perform better and achieve success. For example, moderate stress strengthens the connections between your brain neurons, helping you work faster under pressure. So, as long as stress isn’t chronic, it can be a positive force in your life. Keep a daily list. This lesson is only the beginning. You’ll likely exper

modules.name: SummaryStrive for excellence, not perfection.&nbsp...
modules.description: Summary Strive for excellence, not perfection. If perfection is often your goal, you’re putting unnecessary pressure on yourself. Perfection is more about the result, while excellence has to do with the process. When we strive for excellence, we aim to do our best—but understand that mistakes are part of the growth process. So, the next time you find yourself triple-checking your work or getting hung up on minor errors, breathe and let it go. You might not see an immediate shift in your approach and attitude, but you’ll eventually feel less pressure if you keep at it. Caution: Not all stress is bad. The stress that comes from challenging yourself or learning something new is actually beneficial. It can energize you and help you grow beyond your current capabilities. If you avoid all stress, you may find yourself feeling bored, disengaged, and stagnant. Strive for a balance in which you push yourself—but don’t overextend your emotional and physical resources. Pressure is a mental game.

modules.name: SummaryStrive for moderation. You know that s...
modules.description: Summary Strive for moderation . You know that saying “ too much of a good thing ”? That’s often the case with coping. Coping strategies can cross from being healthy to unhealthy when you do too much (or get too much) of something. For example, we need food to survive and stay healthy. But when food becomes a source of comfort, and we overeat, food then moves into the unhealthy zone. So, strive for moderation. If food, sleep, work, or something else becomes a crutch, you need to reevaluate its role in your life. Choose a regular time to meditate. It’s easier to make meditation a habit if you meditate at the same time every day. For example, you might decide to meditate first thing in the morning, over lunch, or as a way to decompress immediately after returning home from work. Choose a time that’s most beneficial or convenient for you. Switch it up. Try switching up your exercise routines to make it more interesting and exciting. modules.name: SummarySmall steps can make a world of difference....
modules.description: Summary Small steps can make a world of difference. It may seem daunting to address hacking and security breaches, but these small steps can often save you from big trouble down the road. In most cases, thieves will target a house with a single, easy-to-break lock rather than a home with multiple levels of security. Convenience vs. consequence. Our propensity for comfort can often lead to unforeseen consequences. When surfing the internet, take a moment to question whether a convenient function is really worth the potential security risk. Keep your guard up. A thoughtful pause to evaluate your surroundings before connecting to a public network can help you avoid getting hacked. Use common sense when you’re working away from home or the office. If something feels unsafe, trust your gut. Watch your webcam. Many social platforms enable users to share live or pre-recorded videos—or they allow you to video chat with friends. Be careful when using these functions. Hackers can hijack webcams

modules.name: SummaryWhen in doubt, default to confidentiality.&...
modules.description: Summary When in doubt, default to confidentiality. Not sure if something’s supposed to be a secret? Err on the side of caution, and assume it is. That way, you can avoid accidentally disclosing something that shouldn’t be public. Tip: You may have heard people say that writing down your passwords is a bad idea, but some security experts say this process can be safer than using a password manager. There are a few caveats. Don’t write down your passwords at work; just at home. And always keep passwords you’ve written down in a secure location that only you know about. A note on two-factor authentication. You may have heard of two-factor authentication , also known as 2FA, which is a subset of multifactor authentication. A website with 2FA has limited verification to two identifying factors, whereas a multifactor approach can encompass more variables. Remember, when it comes to authentication, more means safer. Tip: All employees should be prepared to respond to both intentional and unint

modules.name: SummaryA healthy dose of distrust can be a good th...
modules.description: Summary A healthy dose of distrust can be a good thing. Do you ever feel obligated to be friendly to strangers? Many of us readily give away details about our lives to people we don’t know. Unfortunately, this can make us vulnerable to social engineering and other scams. While being friendly is fine, know where to draw the line. Ask yourself the questions we covered in this lesson, and don’t be afraid to say no or withdraw from a situation if you feel uncomfortable. Phishing isn’t isolated to email. Scammers use schemes via telephone calls (referred to as vishing, a combination of voice and phishing) and through text messages (referred to as smishing, a combination of SMS and phishing) to perpetrate attacks. No matter the medium, always be wary of unsolicited communications that feature phishing red flags. Report BEC attacks. Tell your boss and IT department about attempted or successful cyberattacks immediately. They will help you determine the next best steps to minimize the damage.

Excessive worry and fear that danger is always around the corner might indicate generalized anxiety disorder. This disorder can be diagnosed, treated, and monitored by a qualified medical professional. Build your EQ score. EQ stands for Emotional Quotient or Emotional Intelligence. Slowing down and thinking through your feelings, motivations, and responses will make you more self-aware and able to help others, too. Reserve judgement on grief. People grieve differently, so give yourself—and everyone else—permission to do it however it feels healthy and productive. Scrutinizing your or someone else’s relationship to the deceased or questioning feelings of grief is unproductive and hurtful. Check if your company offers mental health benefits. modules.description: Summary Take time for rest and recovery. Pushing yourself beyond your physical limits—or failing to take time to rest and recover after intense exercise sessions—can harm your health and make maintaining your plan difficult. Rest and recovery are essential to a well-rounded workout routine, so build them into your plan. Reward yourself. Reinforce your small successes with small treats. Maybe it's a coffee after a tough workout or a massage at the end of the first month of achieving mini-goals. These little celebrations will keep you motivated on the way to the bigger target. Continue modifying your FITT plan. Remember that you’ll need to adjust your exercise plan over time. Avoid pushing yourself too hard, and monitor your progress. If things become too easy or boring, change the FITT components to keep you healthy and interested in your routine. Get out of your comfort zone. Exercise can be challenging, in more ways than one. But the fulfillment and gratification of testing your limit

modules.name: SummaryEmbrace significant life changes. Swit...
modules.description: Summary Embrace significant life changes. Switching jobs? Moving to a new house or city? Becoming a parent? Big life transitions change your environment and context, making it a prime time to break old habits. Change isn’t “one and done.” For changes to last, we must actively work at them. Barriers to change don’t simply disappear; we have to continue to work around them to maintain new habits. Remember, everyone slips up, so don’t feel bad about stops and starts. The key is not giving up. Fortify your morning routine with your nighttime routine. Streamline your mornings by laying out clothes, prepping food, or looking to the next day’s tasks the night before. Feeling prepared for the next day may also help you get better sleep. Your morning routine will thank you! If you’re having consistent trouble sleeping, consider talking to a doctor. Sometimes a lack of quality sleep can be associated with more serious health concerns. If adding an evening routine isn’t working, consult with a me

modules.name: SummaryUse your position to cultivate safety.&nbsp...
modules.description: Summary Use your position to cultivate safety. Employees will keep their thoughts, needs, and emotions to themselves if they feel they will be exploited, ignored, or bullied. If you want your team to be open with you, you have to create a workplace where they feel safe to do so. You are not alone. Raising awareness at work reduces the stigma of mental health that prevents so many people from getting help. It starts by being vocal about your own struggles but also advocating for effective policies that make a difference. When you support your co-workers, you support yourself and pave the way for greater progress. The Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) in the United States operates a hotline for people struggling with substance abuse or addiction. Call 1-800-662-HELP for assistance, information, and referrals 24/7. SAMHSA also provides free resources and guidance for employers on their website at samsha.gov. modules.description: Summary Note: If you find yourself unable to cultivate happiness or struggle with consistent feelings of hopelessness or despair, it may be time to consult a professional to help you find ways to address issues preventing you from achieving overall well-being. What’s one thing you can do? Think back on what you learned in this lesson. We covered a lot of tips, but you don’t need to take them on board all at once. Ask yourself, “What’s one thing I can do today?” Take small, deliberate steps to build fulfilling connections gradually. Give yourself (and others) a break. When you fall into negative patterns, take a moment to think of one thing that makes you grateful for yourself, others, or the world. Moments of gratitude can lift your mood and counterbalance the negative emotion or belief. Seek help from a trained professional. modules.name: SummarySometimes we discover that a job just isn’t...
modules.description: Summary Sometimes we discover that a job just isn’t the right fit—maybe the culture isn’t what you expected, or the compensation doesn’t support your personal goals. In this case, it may be time to consider a career switch. Purpose is a moving target. It changes over time as your values and circumstances change. Check in with yourself regularly to stay connected to what matters most—and give yourself permission to let your purpose evolve. Try something new. You might be the life of the party or the first to leave. Or you might love hearing stories but feel it’s too risky to share about yourself. Maybe you’ve never had a conversation with Gary from accounting. Whatever your habits, shake them up and see what surprises unfold. The best habits are the ones that work for you. Not every habit will be right for you, and that’s OK. If a habit doesn’t resonate with you, is hard to stick to, or doesn’t have a positive impact, ditch it and move on to one that does. modules.description: Summary To create a thriving DIBs environment, your employees must also feel like they belong. Take a look around your workplace. Conduct anonymous surveys. Does everyone have a strong sense of belonging? If not, your diversity and inclusion efforts will continue falling short. Cultivating DIBs through “belonging moments” is a long-term strategy. A single inclusionary moment isn’t enough. You must continue to share stories, listen to others, and ask questions. Every day, try to do one thing on this list. Reach out to others, help them feel included, and make belonging a daily workplace reality. Diversity recruiting is about awareness. Take a look at where you’re falling short. How can you improve your company image? What biases are getting in the way? Once you’re aware of where you need to improve, you can then take action to attract and hire a wider range of applicants. modules.name: SummaryGet to know your people. The role of an HR ...
modules.description: Summary Get to know your people. The role of an HR professional starts with people. Get to know your employees’ skill sets, strengths, goals, backgrounds, challenges, and preferences. Create personal connections with staff and learn about their interests and what they care about. The more in tune you are with your company’s staff, the more likely you are to understand and support their needs. Involve other employees in the process. Consider involving soon-to-be coworkers in the resume screening and interview process. For example, you might add a final interview between top candidates and those who will be working closest with the chosen candidate. That approach gives both sides a chance to get to know who they might be working with and develop a better sense of the workplace culture fit. Keep your reviews consistent. When reviewing resumes/CV, it’s tempting to get distracted by extra details, such as if you went to the same school as an applicant or have a similar hobby. But that infor

modules.name: SummaryPlan for potential interruptions. Schedule ...
modules.description: Summary Plan for potential interruptions. Schedule a 15-minute buffer before and after each interview to protect your interview time from meetings that run late and to avoid having to cut your interview short if it goes long. Also, if you’re conducting a video interview, be sure to test your technology beforehand so that everything runs smoothly when you need it. Don’t hesitate to keep phone interviews short. If it’s the very first interview and you still have a large pool of candidates to narrow down, you might schedule 20-30-minute calls to make the first cut. Don’t let technical issues interrupt your video interviews. Choose and test your video conferencing software well before reaching out to candidates. Provide candidates with details about how to use the program and what to expect. Also, ask candidates to test their tech in advance so that you can both be present for the entirety of your scheduled time together. Try to make a capability test as relevant and accurate as possible.

modules.name: SummaryTake control of what you can. There are man...
modules.description: Summary Take control of what you can. There are many unknowns in life. You don’t know what the job market will be like when you’re older, nor do you know what your health will be like or what government programs will or won’t exist. But there is something that you can guarantee, and that’s saving for your retirement. So, do your future self a favor and commit to taking control of what you can—starting today. You can choose more than one plan. Are you having trouble deciding? Keep in mind that you don’t have to limit yourself to only one retirement plan. Depending on your situation, you may be able to choose more than one option. And you may turn to different plans at different stages of your life. What matters most is that you pick the plan or plans that will work best for your current and anticipated future needs. Don’t compare yourself to others. While each retirement-planning milestone represents a worthy goal, don’t get caught up in the comparisons. We’re all running our own race a

modules.name: SummaryWhat are your biases? When evaluating uncon...
modules.description: Summary What are your biases? When evaluating unconscious bias, the question is not, “Do I have them?” but rather, “What are they?” Remember, unconscious biases are unintentional, so maintain self-awareness. Notice when you’re making judgments too quickly and focus on re-evaluating your thought process. Ask why. Think about past decisions that you’ve made at work—even those that are seemingly inconsequential. Ask yourself: Why did I make that decision? What assumptions was I operating within at that time? Check your assumptions. The next time you jump to conclusions, ask yourself why. Why do you think this? Is this decision based on an irrational bias? Keep your assumptions in check, and you’ll build awareness of your biases, which is the first step to overcoming them. Increase the diversity of your hiring panel. Encourage employees to complete the survey by scheduling it on their calendars. Consider sending a company-wide calendar invite that blocks out 15 minutes for completing the survey during the workday. Set a reminder notification of the appointment and don’t forget to include the link to the survey! Ask your team. Feeling stuck? Unsure which ideas your employees would like best? modules.description: Summary Check your tangible brand elements against your intangible brand. Is there a disconnect, or do they match? For example, a playful tween fashion brand might choose bright logo elements and avoid dour colors, while a federally insured financial institution might consider a formal logo and colors. Ask a friend for feedback. Whether you’re struggling to identify traits that fit or 100% confident in your choices—it’s still worth getting a second opinion. Ask: “What’s your experience of the brand?” or, “What adjectives would you use to describe us?” If your answers are dramatically different from theirs, revisit your self-assessment. Don’t forget about white space. As you build your brand visual design, remember to leave room for those elements to breathe, known as “white space.” Feeling stuck? Try audience personas. modules.description: Summary Make it an ongoing effort. As your company grows and evolves, so will your customers and market. Continue to monitor your target market and track changes through ongoing research, such as customer surveys and sales data. Update your target market and marketing strategy as needed. Avoid stereotypes and assumptions and stick to data. This is no time for guesswork or wild embellishing. Your persona’s passion for gardening is relevant if you’re selling a revolutionary weeding tool—not gourmet hot dogs. Monitor your competition on an ongoing basis. Markets and competitive landscapes continually change. Keep an eye on your top three to five competitors over time by monitoring their social media accounts, website, and other marketing channels. modules.name: SummaryNever buy email lists. Purchasing an email ...
modules.description: Summary Never buy email lists. Purchasing an email list and sending unsolicited messages may seem like an easy way to fast-track building your subscriber list, but it can harm your brand in the long run. Sending messages only to those individuals who opt in and are eager to hear from you will garner better results. Remember, a smaller list of high-quality leads is better than an extensive list of uninterested (and likely annoyed) recipients. Cherish your email lists and work to grow them. Other communication platforms like social media are largely out of your control. Your subscribers and followers could be banned or deleted, or the entire platform could go away. But your email list will always belong to you. Set a calendar reminder now to review spam filter rules. Since rules change frequently, a reminder will keep frequent rule checks from slipping your mind—and keep you ahead of gatekeepers. Test your subject lines. Predicting which technique will work for a given situation isn’t al

modules.description: Summary Successful marketing connects the right customers to your offering. This is accomplished not by finding the customers to suit the offering but by creating an offering that fits the target customers’ wants and needs. The examples of traditional and digital marketing methods in this lesson aren’t meant to be an exhaustive list. Marketing has a rich history of innovation and features a spectrum of methods and strategies to engage customers. The key is finding the right tools for your business. Expanding the 4 Ps. Some marketing professionals use the “7 Ps,” which include additional elements like people, process, and physical evidence. As you consider your offering, you may want to incorporate more Ps to test your marketing strategy further. Winners sometimes quit. Remember, marketing plans are fluid documents for a reason. modules.description: Summary Incorporate analytics as an ongoing part of your marketing strategy and decision-making process. Make a habit of regularly reviewing the data throughout the month—not just at the end of a campaign or reporting period. You may want to hire an expert. Marketing analytics can be overwhelming, and specialists have the knowledge and experience to develop a unique plan for your organization. Instead of going forward alone, consider bringing someone onto your team. Share your findings with other teams. Never place blame on anyone. Instead, keep communication open and work together to improve numbers. There are many advanced methods and models for analyzing campaign effectiveness. We’ve just scratched the surface of what’s possible for you and your marketing plans. Models such as multi-touch attribution, marketing mix modeling, and economic modeling provide deeper insight into different types of campaigns and definitions of success. Knowing what your customers value and how to meet their needs with your offerings will set each product up for success in the marketplace. Lean on the sales team. They’re on the front lines and hear from customers daily. They know what customers think. Ask, “What makes customers excited about this product?” and “What do they wish we did better?” Use these insights to get a more straightforward path. Think ahead. Global brands stay in this phase for decades by predicting what’s next and preparing. Always keep one eye on the market. What trends or services might render you obsolete? Strengthen your position or move to a new one. Use time and resources to your advantage. Especially while you’re still profitable, take time to plan your next move. Meet customer needs as you grow. Software platforms can assist with critical tasks like keyword research and link building. Just make sure the platform you select has a good reputation for quality work. Don’t forget localized keywords. Many businesses only operate in certain geographical areas. You can incorporate local keywords like ZIP codes, city or state names, or even neighborhood names to tailor keywords to local consumers. A warning about “black hat” SEO. Black hat SEO is the term for aggressive and ethically questionable SEO practices like keyword stuffing, hiding text, and purchasing backlinks. These practices might get your domain blocked or classified as spam by major search engines. T echnical SEO is a long game. No one masters all aspects overnight. Instead, set a schedule to tackle a problem or two at a time. Then, conduct a regular SEO audit to updat

modules.name: SummaryPromote your website on emails and social m...
modules.description: Summary Promote your website on emails and social media platforms. Link to your website in email campaigns or signatures—and social media profiles or posts. There are many subcategories of landing pages. Certain lead generation pages focus exclusively on capturing email addresses (“squeeze pages”), while others outline pricing or subscription options. Some landing pages are used for purely informational purposes, such as “About Us” or “Thank You” pages, or to promote upcoming events. Long-form landing pages (“explainer pages”) go into much greater detail about the product or service they’re offering. Consider your campaign-specific goal when tailoring your landing page. Boost your page loading speed. Nobody wants to wait endlessly for a page to load. Users have been conditioned to expect instant results, so any lag at all can easily turn site visitors away. The landing page should already be free of clutter and other elements that may cause drag, but you will want to take additional me

modules.name: SummaryGive and take. A negotiation can only ...
modules.description: Summary Give and take. A negotiation can only end successfully when both sides benefit in some way. Empathize with your buyer’s position for a smoother road to agreement. Take notes on your negotiations. Learn from your experiences by jotting down what you observe during negotiations. By identifying what soars—and what flops—you’ll develop a powerful toolbox of techniques. Don’t take things personally. These tactics exist to throw you off your game. Simply accept them—and the heightened emotions that can ensue—as part of the process. Take a deep breath and get down to business. Making a deal is a journey, not a destination. The stereotypical refrain of the sales world is: “Always be closing.” But that doesn’t mean you should push aggressively from the start. Think of each step as a bridge that connects you to your final goal. Solicit feedback. Improve the transition process today by asking Customer Success what they need from Sales. That will also give you an indication of what questio

modules.description: Summary Self-care is essential for salespeople. The field is fun and exciting, but long hours, the pressure to meet goals, and the prevalence of rejection can lead to burnout. Make sure that as you take care of your customers, you also take care of yourself. Let your pipeline be a helpful tool rather than a rigid structure. Every customer is different, and every solution is unique. So, it’s vital to see contacts as people rather than as figures on a chart. Remember—sales is about building relationships, not tracking stages. A CRM system is only as good as the data a salesperson enters into it. Every member of a sales team needs appropriate training to maintain records within the CRM system. It’s easy to get overwhelmed by all the tools available. Remember that sales tools are only meant to enhance your sales process, not overtake it. Ultimately, sales continues to be about building positive relationships, which good tools can make easier for your team. Alignment between sales and marke

modules.name: SummaryListen closely when customers ask, “Why.”&n...
modules.description: Summary Listen closely when customers ask, “ Why .” Questions that begin with “why” are often sales objections in disguise. For example, a customer who asks, “Why is this option so much more expensive?” may be expressing an objection to the product’s price. Pay attention—and treat disguised sales objections accordingly. Be selfless. At the core of each objection strategy is selflessness. A naysaying prospect has their guard up. So, if you want to move past that barrier, you must prove that your motives are genuine. Regardless of the objection, be selfless. Communicate to your prospect that you’re not just trying to land the sale—you’re trying to help them with the best solution. Aim for a mixed approach. We covered a lot of techniques in this lesson. And, while you might already have a favorite, never rely on just one. Every prospect is different, which means there’s no one way to solve every problem. So, concentrate on combining the techniques and aiming for a mixed approach—as that w

modules.name: SummaryAsk for honest feedback. Especially if...
modules.description: Summary Ask for honest feedback. Especially if this is your first leadership role, be open to honest feedback from your team and management colleagues. Honest feedback can be uncomfortable in the short term—but it will help you improve in the long run. Hold yourself accountable. Ultimately, your actions will make or break your team’s success. So, be sure you’re giving your team the best chance to excel. To measure your effectiveness, meet with sales reps individually and ask what’s working and what’s not. Or, ask a supervisor or mentor for an honest assessment of your performance. Is one methodology enough for your team? Probably not. No single strategy works in every situation. As your company grows and business needs evolve, so will the methods you use to close sales. What worked in one stage of your company’s growth may fall flat in another. So, arm your team with several effective methodologies and adapt your approach when needed. Which department owns sales enablement? Confusion s

modules.name: SummaryCreate and use proposal templates. The...
modules.description: Summary Create and use proposal templates. They make customization easy and can save you time and effort when putting together your next document. Consider saving drafts of your proposals to use as templates for future projects. Don’t read directly from your notes. Aim to engage your audience in conversation. Use your notes and visual aids as guides to help you keep your audience’s attention. Get creative! Like Grace, you aren’t bound to a presentation template. As long as you’re keeping your audience and their expectations in mind, you can push the boundaries of traditional presentations. Doing so can make you stand out even more. Email a copy of your slide deck after the presentation. This gives you an opportunity to follow up with the prospect and remind them of your solution’s selling points. Communicate with certainty. modules.description: Summary Create a consistent schedule. Successful prospecting takes consistency and perseverance. To stay on track, develop a weekly plan that allows time for refining your target list, researching potential buyers, and reaching out regularly. Track your progress. Success is about trial and error, so get in the habit of recording your results. How many times did you contact prospect A before they answered? What outreach methods did you use? What about prospect B, C, and D? Record your results, and you can create the perfect outreach plan for you. Never stop analyzing. Studying your competition isn’t a one-time event. New competitors will continue to pop up—and minor threats may adjust their positioning and become significant contenders. Don’t get sideswiped by market changes. Reevaluate your competition regularly and continue refining your sales strategy, so you stand out in the crowd. Practice, practice, practice. Perfecting the cold call process takes time. Don’t get discouraged if yo

modules.description: Summary No matter what, don’t stretch the truth just to make a sale. Understanding buyer psychology is useful when your product or service fits a customer’s needs. But it’s irrelevant if what you’re selling wouldn’t meet the buyer’s real need. Get out of your comfort zone occasionally. That doesn’t mean forcing yourself to become someone you’re not. However, there’s value in expanding your horizons by practicing skills that don’t come naturally to you. Think like a champion. Is there a sports figure you admire? Or someone else you respect who embodies resilience? When champions fall, they brush it off and get back up. So, the next time someone rejects you, do the same—get back in the game. Get comfortable. Communicating nonverbally may not come easily at first. Practice until it feels natural to use open hand gestures, nod frequently, and close wide physical gaps when speaking to others. You’ll be an expert in body language before you know it. Which style is the most challenging? Certa

modules.description: Summary What do I do with unqualified leads? If a contact’s needs seem like they won’t ever align, you can refer them to another company or solution. But if the contact has the potential to become qualified over time, then feed them back into your lead nurturing cycle until the time is right. Ask why. Sometimes a potential buyer may struggle to communicate the root of their needs. For example, they might say they have a specific requirement (such as a five-star user rating)—which is actually the symptom of a deeper underlying need (dependability). In these cases, it helps to ask, “Why?” For example, “ Why is a five-star user rating important to you?” Prepare your questions. A handy list of common questions you can use to get to know prospective customers will help you remember what to ask when you’re talking with someone. Go above and beyond to help potential buyers . Extraordinary customer service can set your company apart and demonstrate your genuine desire to add value to their buy

modules.name: SummaryDon’t skip any steps. It’s tempting to...
modules.description: Summary Don’t skip any steps. It’s tempting to jump ahead when implementing change. But if steps are skipped, and changes aren’t well-thought-out, you risk being worse off than when you originally started. Take the time to follow every process step, and you’ll improve your chances of success. Don’t skip the impact analysis. All these steps may seem time-consuming (especially when you’re busy), but they’re worth the extra attention. If you skip the impact analysis, you open the door to surprises that could disrupt your entire project. Invest the time today, and you’ll save even more time tomorrow. Maintain consistent communication. Communication is your most powerful tool. As a best practice, don’t want until there’s a change to start the dialogue. Maintain consistent communication throughout your project, and you’ll already have engaged stakeholders you can turn to for sponsorship and buy-in. Review. Revise. Improve. Change doesn’t stop once it’s approved. You must continue to review c

Make sure your team understands what you expect of them. Then, as the project progresses, don’t close anyone out. Keep communication constant. It’s the key to success. You set the tone. As the project manager, you set the tone. Lead by example by encouraging your team and stakeholders to share their thoughts, ideas, and concerns. Be the type of manager who sets the right environment of honesty, respect, and collaboration, and others will follow. How important is your message? When considering the best method and tool for your situation, ask yourself: How important is this message? If my receiver doesn’t read it, will that negatively impact the project? modules.name: SummaryGive it time. Some project management ...
modules.description: Summary Give it time. Some project management frameworks require more time to learn than others. Give yourself time to learn a framework, and then train your team. You will likely experience some level of trial and error along the way. Learn from your mistakes, and look for new ways to refine your process on every project. The framework will eventually become second nature. Consider your project needs. Consider the unique nature and requirements of your project to help decide if Waterfall, Agile, or Lean are for you. If none of these frameworks fit the bill, then head online and research the many alternative frameworks out there. Work backward. Even though it makes sense to start from the beginning when defining project tasks, it’s actually more helpful to start from the end of a project when developing your schedule. Start with the project’s deadline and work backward from there. Make your board visible. Whether you’re using a Kanban board or a Scrum task board, make it highly visible

modules.description: Summary Resolving issues. While it’s best to avoid these mistakes altogether, don’t panic if you’ve already made one or two of them. Instead, go back. See if there’s an implementation step you missed. Remember the tools and techniques you have at your disposal and focus on resolving the issue as quickly as possible. Focus on what you can do, not what you can’t. Another strategy for challenging client requests is to focus on what you can do instead of what you can’t. Offer a better or alternative option that still addresses the client’s problem. Send your client a post-project feedback survey. Consider sending your client a survey or questionnaire after the project is over with a few simple questions such as, “In what areas did we meet or exceed your expectations?” and “In what areas could we improve?” A post-project survey will help you improve how you handle projects in the future. modules.description: Summary Hold a scheduling team meeting. One of the most challenging steps in developing your project schedule is estimating task durations, especially when you don’t have any experience with the tasks yourself. Schedule a meeting with your project team and ask for their input. You’ll likely get a more realistic idea of how long a task will take. You can also account for timing conflicts like vacation days or competing deadlines for other projects. Use verbs for activities and nouns for deliverables. Project activities are action items, whereas project deliverables are outcomes. So, use verbs when listing project activities and nouns for listing tangible project deliverables. Create a time log. No matter which time estimation technique you use, you can empower your team to make more accurate time estimates by creating a time log. modules.description: Summary Get familiar with different reports. There are several types of project reports, and the best project managers will familiarize themselves with all of them. That way, you can effectively monitor progress and make informed, fact-based decisions. Keep it short and simple. There’s a lot of information that goes into a status report, but do your best to avoid long narratives. By limiting your report to four sections and keeping the text concise, it’s less likely that crucial information will be missed. Remember, a status report is a snapshot, not an essay. Be wary of positive cost variances. Often, project managers worry about being over budget when identifying deviations. But remember to consider all variances—both positive and negative. While being under budget might be the result of a savvy cost-saving measure, it could also mean that you’re the victim of schedule lag or ineffective estimates. Always stick to the plan. modules.description: Summary Communicate how costs often increase with time. Adding new features or implementing changes to project scope often become more difficult and costly the later they’re submitted. Communicate this relationship to your client. The earlier you can catch scope changes, the easier they are to implement. The later you submit a change during a project’s execution, the more complex and expensive the changes—and your project—may become. Create a prototype. For some projects, it may be helpful to create a prototype or sample of the project to gather your client’s or customer’s initial thoughts, feedback, and requirements. Start by asking the right questions. If you find yourself struggling to articulate the project scope, then you might not have taken enough time to talk to your client and deeply understand their needs, goals, and expectations for the project. Set up a meeting to explore together what a project does and doesn’t include. Ask targeted questions to find out how your client de

Prompting customers this way can leave a positive impression even if you don’t hear back. Shortcuts are your best friend. Create keyboard shortcuts for common responses to save yourself some time. You can always add small variations to the auto-filled text to customize interactions. Beware of trolls. Not every complaint will be genuine. “Trolls” seek to sow chaos and discord online. Some people simply will not accept your answers, and you have no obligation to put up with mistreatment or abuse. Ask for help, and take care of yourself! Customer service is within your control. You can’t manage your customers’ experience with other brands, but you can control how they see you and your business. A friendly, thoughtful experience will encourage your customers to keep coming back for more. Reflect on your conversations. Self-reflection is a great tool for improving your active listening. The next time you work with a customer, reflect on the interaction when you’ve finished. Did anything distract you? Did you jump to any conclusions? Get to know your disrupting patterns, and you’ll take the first step toward breaking them. What if the customer doesn’t want help? Even the best greetings won’t work all the time. modules.description: Summary Developing cultural sensitivity is an ongoing effort. It’s not a one-time event. Treat this lesson as a starting point for ongoing research, learning, self-evaluation, and conversation. Observe and follow the customer’s lead. Simply knowing about and celebrating cultural differences will boost your competence as a customer service professional. So, don’t stress if you’re unsure of a customer’s cultural norms or preferences. Take your cues from the customer on communication style, body language, and behaviors, then follow their lead Be supportive. Cross-cultural communication seeks to make all parties feel comfortable. Approach cross cultural communications with an attitude of encouragement, patience, and sensitivity. Always treat customers with respect. Check your affinity bias. We tend to associate more with people who are similar to us—from the same culture, age group, or social class, for example. Notice when you feel more comfortable with certain groups of people over other

modules.description: Summary Pause before responding to upset customers. Whether you take a deep breath or count to three, pausing can interrupt your body’s natural “fight or flight” response to stressful situations. That gives you time to collect your thoughts and avoid reacting emotionally. Always get help if you feel unsafe. The above steps are good general rules to follow. However, if you ever feel unsafe, end the conversation immediately—and get help. And, if you’re interacting in person, call security. Your safety matters most. It has nothing to do with you. If you find yourself getting upset while dealing with an emotional customer, remind yourself: “This has nothing to do with me.” An upset customer is not the result of your failings. Numerous factors are working outside your control. So, take a deep breath and keep your attention on protecting yourself and moving forward. Learn from your mistakes. Treat every mistake as a learning opportunity. What can you do to avoid similar mistakes in the futur

modules.name: SummaryContact center vs. call center. Many people...
modules.description: Summary Contact center vs. call center. Many people use these terms interchangeably, but contact centers offer customers various ways to reach out for support (known as multichannel customer service). Call centers, on the other hand, field phone calls only. Should you outsource your contact center instead? If you’re short on time and resources, outsourcing your contact center may be a better option than building and maintaining an in-house team. Outsourcing can be more affordable, and it allows you to scale easily. Keep in mind, however, that the quality of customer interactions can take a hit with outsourcing. So, be sure to weigh the pros and cons of each option before moving forward. View your strategy as a work in progress. No matter how confident you feel in your finished customer service strategy, always view it as a work in progress. Changing consumer demands, new competitors, and other factors can influence how your team approaches customer interactions. Be ready to pivot when

modules.description: Summary Development is a journey. Developing your strengths takes time. The process is a journey that requires ongoing work. So, make those daily reflections a habit. Continue to think about how you can better use your strengths and stay persistent—because practice makes perfect. Every matching career won’t be right. There’s a lot of variety when it comes to matching careers and interests. Some jobs will pique your interests immediately, while others will feel off-base. Trust your instincts. Remember, you’re exploring what you enjoy. So, if something doesn’t relate to your other interest areas, remove it from your list of options. Values change with time. Your values define who you are in your life today—and they may change as you move through different stages. So, continue to assess your values. And if you’re ever feeling unhappy with your job, do another self-assessment and ask yourself: “Is my job honoring my core values?” If not, commit to changing course. Are you a remote worker?

modules.name: SummaryTalk to a manager or mentor. While you...
modules.description: Summary Talk to a manager or mentor. While you should be the one driving your career, it can also be helpful to get another person’s perspective or have someone to help hold you accountable. Talk to your manager or a mentor about your professional goals and interests. Ask for a second opinion on what they see as your strengths. And get their input on opportunities to develop the skills or gain the experience you need to move forward. Review and update your career development plan regularly. Set up recurring meetings with your manager to report on your progress and revise your career development plan. That may mean you meet to review your plan every month, once a quarter, twice a year or annually. It’s up to you and your manager. Having regular meetings will ensure that you don’t go months without making progress toward your career goals—and that your manager is invested the process, too. Give examples. Give specific examples when sharing constructive feedback with your manager. Pinpoin

modules.name: SummaryStay on good terms. If you decide that...
modules.description: Summary Stay on good terms. If you decide that it’s time to find a new job, avoid burning bridges on your way out. Give appropriate notice. Have a plan in place. And do everything you can to make the transition smooth for your former boss and team. They may be important contacts to have in your network for landing the position that is right for you. Allocate space on your resume by level of importance. For example, allocate more space to describe your most recent job experience and less space to describe a less relevant internship. Pay attention to how much visual space each section of your resume consumes. Talk about what you can offer the job—instead of what the job can offer you. Sure, employers like to know why the job appeals to you or why you’re passionate about your work—but that shouldn’t take over your entire cover letter. Don’t spend too much time on your cover letter gushing about why the job is ideal for you. Remember, your goal is to communicate why you’d be ideal for the

modules.name: SummaryBe a model mentee. To increase your ch...
modules.description: Summary Be a model mentee. To increase your chances of landing a mentor, make sure that you’re an enjoyable mentee. Be open, optimistic, and enthusiastic. Always demonstrate an exemplary work ethic, and don’t get defensive when receiving feedback. Express your appreciation of your mentor’s time by being flexible and accommodating their schedule. Add value. Your mentor has their own reasons for mentoring you. They may be hoping to hone their interpersonal skills, fill a business need, enhance their job satisfaction, or gain recognition from their peers. How can you make the mentoring relationship valuable for them? Whether it’s talking your mentor up to peers or offering respectful feedback on their mentoring techniques, look for ways that you can add value for your mentor, too. Are you ready to be a mentor? Determine if you’re prepared to be a mentor by answering the following questions: Do you have the time and energy to commit to regular mentoring sessions? Do you enjoy helping other

modules.name: SummaryStart today. The worst networking mist...
modules.description: Summary Start today. The worst networking mistake you can make is putting it off. Genuine connections take time to cultivate and maintain. So, if you wait for a crisis to kick-start your networking, you’ll be unlikely to reap rewards. Start growing your network today, and you can leverage those contacts when you need them. Treat connections like friendships. A networking event isn’t an interview. Your goal isn’t to sell yourself—it’s to create personal connections. So, ask yourself, “If this person was a friend, What would I say?” Remember, a great professional relationship is built on honesty and trust—just like friendship. You don’t need to share everything. It’s tempting to share your life story, but an elevator pitch should be brief. Time yourself, continue making revisions, and confirm that your speech is no more than 60 seconds long. Remember, It’s better to leave people wanting more than it is to overwhelm them with details. If they’re curious, they’ll ask questions. Start where

modules.name: SummaryNobody’s perfect. Everyone slips up—an...
modules.description: Summary Nobody’s perfect. Everyone slips up—and that’s OK. You can’t be expected to know everything on day one. It takes time to settle into a new job. So approach this week as an exciting challenge and chance to demonstrate your best qualities. Don’t skip the plan . A 30/60/90-day plan does take work to set up—but the focus and autonomy that will result from a well-developed plan make it well worth the effort. You already have great ideas from your reflections in this course—now, turn your notes into your recipe for success! Harness the power of “yet.” Try adding the word “yet” to make any negative statement positive. “I don’t know how to do that” has potential when you say, “I don’t know how to do that, yet.” Pause and take a breath. Before you take credit, pause and ask yourself, “Who else deserves credit?” Before you say “Yes,” take a breath and ask yourself, “What skills do I contribute to this project?” If you don’t like an idea immediately, pause and ask yourself, “Why don’t I l

modules.name: SummaryThis is temporary. As you navigate the...
modules.description: Summary This is temporary. As you navigate these new waters, keep reminding yourself that this is all temporary. You won’t be unemployed forever, and you can pick yourself up. Losing your job is what you make of it. So, avoid falling into that “woe is me” mentality and, instead, keep your perspective looking forward. Find the positives. Often, we learn the most from our failures, not our successes. So, if you’re feeling down on yourself, consider the benefits of your mishap. How will this mistake help you grow and improve? Use mistakes as a form of personal development, and you can shift your perspective and start to see the silver lining. Don’t wait it out. Low motivation rarely solves itself. More often than not, it only escalates with time. So, if you’re in a motivation rut, don’t wait it out. Don’t hope things will get better. Instead, take action because you’re the only person in control of what happens next. Give it time. As the five reaction stages prove, accepting change takes

modules.name: SummaryReview your company’s code of conduct.&nbsp...
modules.description: Summary Review your company’s code of conduct. A code of conduct provides more tailored guidelines for professional behaviors in your workplace. Send a copy to yourself. Send a copy of important emails to yourself to file for future reference. Avoid using speakerphone when it’s not necessary. While it may seem tempting to have your hands free to type or sort papers while you talk, the quality of your call diminishes. Callers may have trouble hearing over speakerphone due to echo or background noise. Worse—they may wonder if you’re paying attention. When in doubt, err on the side of formality. Start formal, and take the other party’s lead from there. You can always change your tone to mirror the other person’s response.

modules.name: SummaryMake a habit of asking yourself, “What...
modules.description: Summary Make a habit of asking yourself, “What do my coworkers need from me today?” Whether they need a word of encouragement, a second set of eyes on a project, or someone who can take a task off their plate and run with it, do everything in your power to fulfill that need for them. Ask your boss for regular feedback. If you want to know how you could be a better employee, why not ask your boss directly? Your boss will appreciate your initiative, and you can glean some helpful tips to develop as an individual and improve your professional relationship. Don’t stop working on your professional relationship after you’ve repaired the damage. Continue to develop your interpersonal skills and be careful to maintain the trust and goodwill that you’ve worked so hard to restore. Talk to your manager if you’ve exhausted other options. modules.name: SummaryPay attention to how individuals are handli...
modules.description: Summary Pay attention to how individuals are handling change. Some employees may be excited about change, while others feel hesitant, fearful, or downright angry. When managing the people side of change, remember that there is no one-size-fits-all solution. A human-centered approach to change is rooted in equity and empathy—and that looks different for everyone. Check your bias. As you plan small or expansive changes, play devil’s advocate. What are you assuming about the change that may not be true? This line of questioning may free up ideas, resources, and innovations that otherwise might get overlooked. Don’t go it alone. Change is inevitable—which also means it’s been done countless times before. A proven model can guide you. Think about the support you need, assess the different models, and choose ones that’ll help you reach those goals. Always inform people to the extent possible. One of the pitfalls of change is the lack of clarity about why change is needed and how it will happ

modules.name: SummaryRecognition is a helpful motivator. As...
modules.description: Summary Recognition is a helpful motivator. As the initiative progresses, acknowledge team members modeling behaviors you want. Try a mix of rewards: Spotlight team members, offer financial bonuses, and award promotions or new responsibilities. This will encourage other employees to follow suit. Give it time. Lewin’s model follows a slow and steady pace. The framework only works if you give employees time to adjust. Remember, your team needs to become familiar with a change before initiation. And they need space to practice and make mistakes before refreezing operations. Change management models typically simplify the change process by transforming it into a series of straightforward steps. In reality, change is more complex and messy than models show. The key is to tailor a model to your needs. Maybe your organization needs to invest more time in creating a sense of urgency, or perhaps employee resistance is a barrier requiring additional time to dismantle. The process won’t look the

modules.name: SummaryCultivate healthy communication strategies ...
modules.description: Summary Cultivate healthy communication strategies early. Don’t wait until you get hit with an unexpected change! Start talking about change in consistent, clear ways to prepare your organization for the next transition. When it comes to change leadership, a “we’re all in this together” approach will have better results than an “us versus them” mentality. Focus on teamwork and collaboration, and make sure all employees know they are playing a valued role in the change—and, subsequently, in the organization’s ongoing success. How’s everyone feeling? Nothing scuppers a great plan faster than a mass refusal to adopt it. Implement methods to keep tabs on the team’s emotional temperature. Is it moving in the right direction? If not, consider adjusting tactics.

modules.name: SummaryResearch change leadership models. The...
modules.description: Summary Research change leadership models. There are many different methods and perspectives for leaders regarding change. Explore what’s available and choose the models that best fit your leadership style and team. Don’t forget to celebrate! When you’re focused on overall success, it’s easy to gloss over wins and milestones. But don’t forget that all victories, even small ones, are worth celebrating. Leave space for every voice. Carve out time to hear from every team member. Let them share concerns, and be sure to acknowledge them. It’s OK to respond and gently nudge them in a better direction. Have them repeat your positive interpretation in their own words to be sure they understand it.

modules.name: SummaryFind a motivating story. Keep a stress...
modules.description: Summary Find a motivating story. Keep a stressed team afloat by connecting to a shared or personal vision of the future. When it’s time to make tough decisions or the next step is unclear, return to the story to find meaning and purpose within the crisis. How can you foster your team’s resilience and adaptability together? Resilience, or the ability to cope with difficulty, is an important partner to adaptability. H ow would you rate your team’s resilience and adaptability now? Are they more skilled in one over the other? How can you promote both? Do your performance metrics make sense? As day-to-day work shifts and team members take on new tasks, chances are metrics need adjusting to reflect the rapidly changing situation. Find new ways to recognize and reward teams rising to the challenge. Change your mindset. Instead of viewing change as a “necessary evil,” help your team envision how change can accelerate growth and create a better future.

modules.name: SummaryFoster trust with transparency. Open t...
modules.description: Summary Foster trust with transparency. Open the books and let employees or stakeholders see the data that informed your proposition for change. Let them see for themselves why change is urgent and necessary. Stay on track with frequent gut checks. Ask employees for feedback by asking, “How do you think we’re doing?” and, “What could we do better to reach our vision?” Take their opinions to heart, and show them that you’re listening by promptly responding and implementing what’s feasible. Where are opportunities hiding? Think of the change from multiple perspectives to uncover skill-building, innovation, and efficiency possibilities. You might be surprised by what you find from another point of view! Stay positive, regardless of the outcome. Not all changes will be successful. Sometimes, leadership makes mistakes. They may even have to revise their plan. But even if your skepticism is validated, it shouldn’t change your attitude toward change. Your strategy should remain consistent reg

modules.name: SummaryReport bullying, harassment, or illegal beh...
modules.description: Summary Report bullying, harassment, or illegal behavior. You do not need to handle challenges which involve bullying, harassment, or illegal behavior on your own. Follow company protocol for reporting these behaviors and seeking support from your supervisor or human resources. Ask what they prefer. If you’re unsure what communication style the other person prefers, just ask them directly. For example, you might ask, “What’s your preferred communication style?” Or, “Is there anything I could change or do differently to communicate more effectively?” They’ll appreciate the effort, and you won’t have to guess what works best for them. Keep it confidential. Don’t gossip or discuss issues you’re having with a difficult coworker publicly. Talk directly to your coworker. Keep your complaints between the two of you. The only exception is when dealing with a workplace bully. Document abuse and discuss it with an appropriate supervisor or human resources professional. Don’t leapfrog your boss.

modules.name: SummaryAre you empathetic or sympathetic? If ...
modules.description: Summary Are you empathetic or sympathetic? If you’re unsure whether you’re empathetic or sympathetic, ask yourself: Am I relating personally to this emotion or experience? Do I understand the feeling? If you can step into the other person’s shoes and experience the moment along with them, then you’re showing empathy. Empathy builds understanding. Empathy doesn’t mean that you need to always agree with or please others. Rather, it’s about understanding. By thoughtfully considering how others feel and view things, you’ll be a more effective coworker or leader. Empathy isn’t always about fixing. When communicating empathy, it’s tempting to try to fix the problem. But sometimes, a person just wants to feel understood. They want to feel less alone. Before offering advice, ask the person if they want it: “I hear you, and I’m sorry this is happening to you. Would you like help?” Overcome snap judgements. The next time you find yourself making a snap judgment about another person, try to overr

modules.name: SummarySender tip. The next time you send a m...
modules.description: Summary Sender tip. The next time you send a message, try placing yourself in the receiver’s shoes. If you were receiving this message (instead of sending it), how would you answer the following two questions: “Is the message clear and straightforward?” and “What’s my overall interpretation of this message?” Build relationships from conflict. Conflicts can be nerve-wracking, but they’re also a chance for growth! The next time you experience conflict, take a step back and seek to understand the other person’s perspective. Once you fully understand where they’re coming from, let them know you do. They’ll feel more valued, increasing the chances you’ll have a productive dialogue and resolution. Nonverbal communication: practice makes perfect. Even when we’re silent, our nonverbal cues can speak volumes. If you have an important presentation or conversation coming up, try practicing in front of a mirror or recording yourself. That can help you become more aware of your nonverbal communicat

modules.name: SummaryKeep track of time. One of the biggest...
modules.description: Summary Keep track of time. One of the biggest meeting challenges is ending on time. Navigate this challenge effectively by keeping a clock, stopwatch, or another form of countdown visible. Use a template. Agendas are typically designed as a list or outline and are ideally limited to a single page. However, it may be longer depending on the length and complexity of the meeting. You can save yourself time when developing future meeting agendas by designing a template to use again. Practice active listening. Develop your skills in active listening to better facilitate group meetings. Listen, make eye contact, and use encouraging body language to show that you are listening and engaged in what the speaker is saying. You might also paraphrase what the speaker says and repeat the speaker’s main points back to the group for deeper reflection. Delegate decisions to appropriate stakeholders. Consensus-based decision-making is ideal for meetings in which all participants play a relevant role in

modules.name: SummaryMix it up. Negotiation and persuasion ...
modules.description: Summary Mix it up. Negotiation and persuasion are not mutually exclusive. You can use a persuasive argument to make your case during a negotiation. Or, if you find yourself unable to persuade the other party, you can always resort to negotiation. Don’t hesitate to use both practices if the situation calls for it. Do your homework. Before heading into any negotiation, research the other party to better understand their interests, context, and challenges. Try to prepare mutually beneficial alternatives that might exist in case your offer isn’t accepted. Make the first offer. Making the first offer in a negotiation sets an anchor for the discussion that follows. Take control of the range of the negotiation by making the first move. Brainstorm potential concerns. Before you try to persuade someone, consider all sides of the argument and create a list of potential concerns the other party may have. Not only should you be prepared to dispel these concerns, but you also might consider bringin

modules.description: Summary Boil it down to one sentence. Try to combine your insights and compress your objective into a single sentence. It will be easier to share—and to remember while developing your presentation. Look for gaps. Practice your presentation on someone who’s not familiar with the topic. Do they understand what you’re saying? Can they clearly follow your presentation structure? Ask targeted questions after your practice session to identify gaps where the audience may get lost or confused. Assign one topic per visual aid. Focus each visual aid on communicating just one main idea or topic. Review your presentation outline if you’re having trouble discerning your presentation’s main ideas. Research design tools. There are plenty of free and paid design tools available to use. From Canva, Google Slides, Keynote, and Microsoft PowerPoint for slide deck design—to sites like Pexels and Librestock for stock photography—a little online research may save you time and give you some design inspiratio

modules.name: SummaryPractice makes perfect. Being a great ...
modules.description: Summary Practice makes perfect. Being a great verbal communicator is a skill, and like all skills, it can be learned. Improve your communication skills by finding opportunities to speak up. Pay close attention to what you say and how others interpret that message. Were you successful? Why, or why not? The more you practice your skills and observe your performance, the better you’ll get at it. Who’s your listener? When choosing a conversation starter, consider your listener. If you’re meeting your company’s founder, it’s not the best idea to start with, “What’s your favorite food?” Instead, a better approach is to start with a compliment such as, “I’m so impressed with everything you’ve done. How did you get the idea to create such a unique brand?” Remember: The best conversation starters cater to your listener. Keep your questions on topic. Often, our questions can lead a speaker in a direction they didn’t plan on taking. If you notice that has happened, take ownership and steer the di

modules.name: SummaryWrite down your purpose. Before you wr...
modules.description: Summary Write down your purpose. Before you write your next message, take a moment to ask yourself: “What is my number one goal for communicating this message?” After you answer that question, write your purpose down somewhere so that it’s easy to see. As you write your message, challenge yourself to keep looking back at that purpose. Are you staying on track? Keep your goal in mind, and you’ll then be more likely to meet it. Does the medium fit the message? Before you seal the envelope, print the report, or press “enter” on your keyboard, think it through. Remember, the medium will set the tone of your message. Choose the wrong one, and you could dilute your communication. Consider urgency , formality , and length , and ask yourself: Does this medium work? Your tone can change. Keep in mind that as your relationships change, so can your tone. At the start of most business communications, you’ll want to use a professional tone as a means of showing respect. If you note that the other p

modules.description: Summary Don’t be overconfident. It’s easy to assume that a crisis won’t happen to you—until it does. Don’t leave yourself and your company vulnerable. Make crisis management a priority. Put together a crisis team to get started on crisis prevention and preparation. What could go wrong? An effective strategy for assessing your biggest risk factors is to ask yourself and your crisis team this question: What could go wrong? While it sounds pessimistic, it’s a practical strategy for evaluating the real threats to your business and creating a more optimistic future for your company. Aim to review your plan and conduct crisis training annually. Companies change, just like their environments and risk factors change. You need to review your crisis management plan and conduct crisis training at least once a year to ensure that it’s relevant and your crisis team is adequately prepared. Withdraw advertising that’s inappropriate in light of the crisis. Your crisis team may want to consider if you

modules.name: SummaryEQ starts with you. Emotional intelligence ...
modules.description: Summary EQ starts with you. Emotional intelligence involves several components, but they all start with you. By building your self-awareness, you’ll begin to recognize similar emotions in others. By learning self motivation, you’ll develop strategies for motivating a team. So, start small. Start thinking about your own feelings and reactions, and soon, you’ll improve your ability to understand and influence others. Keep practicing. A onetime reflection or feedback session isn’t enough. To develop self-awareness, you must make a conscious, everyday commitment to practice awareness. So, make a habit of regularly checking in with your experiences, noticing your feelings, and using every moment as an opportunity to learn more about yourself and your emotions. Don’t play the blame game. It’s easy to blame our situations for our reactions: “I’m angry because my staff doesn’t get it!” But remember, you can choose how you react. So, the next time you feel a strong emotion, remember that you’re

modules.name: SummaryInvest in yourself. Effective leadersh...
modules.description: Summary Invest in yourself. Effective leadership skills take time and effort to develop. Invest in accelerating your growth. Find a mentor. Seek out in-person workshops or live seminar trainings. Join a professional group, or, build your library of leadership development books. Encourage employees to be themselves. Leaders may unknowingly create tension and resentment when they expect employees to act the way they do or be motivated by the same factors. Recognize employees for their individuality. Pay less attention to the things that they aren’t doing and more to the things that they’re naturally attracted to, skilled in, or that complement their personality and interests. Encourage employees to be their authentic selves, and then call on their natural strengths to achieve desired outcomes. Be relentless in the pursuit of your vision. Your job isn’t over once you’ve developed your vision statement. It’s just begun. Create a strategy and action plan for realizing it. Communicate your v
